summary:
The Fashoda Incident of 1898 was a tense confrontation between Britain and France at the remote Sudan outpost of Fashoda (modern Kodok) on the White Nile, arising from the collision of competing imperial strategies for Africa. It brought the two countries closer to war than they had been since the Napoleonic era and was ultimately resolved by French diplomatic retreat, significantly shaping the subsequent development of the Anglo-French Entente Cordiale.

The incident arose from the collision of two imperial projects. Britain, following the reconquest of Sudan from the Mahdists at the [[event:2596|Battle of Omdurman]] in September 1898, was asserting control over the Nile Valley. France, pursuing its own African ambitions, had sent Captain Jean-Baptiste Marchand with a small expedition across the continent from the French Congo to establish a French presence on the Upper Nile, hoping to establish a link between France's West African and East African territories and perhaps leverage a Nile foothold to gain concessions elsewhere.

When Kitchener's much larger British-Egyptian force arrived at Fashoda on 19 September 1898, they found Marchand and his small party already there. The two commanders were personally civil, but both claimed the territory for their respective countries. The confrontation became a major diplomatic crisis as Britain refused to negotiate while French forces remained on the Nile and moved warships into position. Faced with Britain's overwhelming military advantage and with no prospect of French naval forces challenging British power in the region, the French government ordered Marchand's withdrawal in November 1898. The episode was a major humiliation for France and definitively established British dominance over the Upper Nile.
